threats.title: Arbitrary NFT Minting via Unsigned Quantity Parameter
threats.hypothesis_description: The signature and Merkle claim functions do not include the mint quantity in the verified payload, allowing attackers to reuse a valid proof for 1 NFT to mint the maximum supply.
threats.investigation_rationale: Cryptographic verification must cover all critical state-changing parameters to prevent payload manipulation and unauthorized supply inflation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mintArgs_.quantity is used for minting but not included in encodeData_ or leafPart_

preconditions: Attacker has a valid signature or Merkle proof for at least 1 NFT

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: MintArgs containing quantity and imageURI is passed alongside the signed payload but not verified by it
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Anytime during the valid minting window

operational_questions: Can an attacker successfully execute a claim with a modified quantity?
Does the factory contract properly enforce maxSupply when quantity is manipulated?
validation_requirements: Test signatureClaim with a valid signature for quantity=1 but passing quantity=100 in mintArgs
Verify if the signer intended to restrict quantity per signature

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain a valid signature or Merkle proof for minting 1 NFT
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Construct a transaction calling signatureClaim or merkleClaim
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Modify the mintArgs_.quantity parameter to equal the remaining maxSupply
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Execute the transaction to mint all remaining NFTs

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete depletion of NFT supply by unauthorized actors
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - minted NFTs cannot be easily revoked

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Single addresses minting large quantities of NFTs in one transaction
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Rapid depletion of NFT max supply
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Mismatch between off-chain intended distribution and on-chain mints

threats.title: Permanent Lock of User Shares via Public Tracking Array Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: Internal array tracking functions are marked public, allowing any attacker to corrupt a victim's cred tracking state and permanently prevent them from selling shares.
threats.investigation_rationale: State tracking arrays must be strictly synchronized with actual balances; exposing their mutation functions publicly breaks the core accounting invariant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Functions _addCredIdPerAddress and _removeCredIdPerAddress are declared public instead of internal

preconditions: Victim owns shares in a cred

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Internal state management functions are exposed to external callers
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Anytime before a victim attempts to sell their shares

operational_questions: Can an external caller successfully remove a credId from another user's tracking array?
Does this removal cause subsequent sell operations by the victim to revert?
validation_requirements: Execute _removeCredIdPerAddress as an attacker targeting a victim
Attempt to sell shares as the victim and observe the revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a victim address holding shares in a specific credId
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: When victim calls sellShareCred, the internal _updateCuratorShareBalance fails to find the index and reverts

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent locking of user funds and shares
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade to fix corrupted state

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users failing to sell shares with WrongCredId or IndexOutofBounds reverts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Targeted griefing of specific high-value accounts

threats.title: Unauthorized Extraction of Protocol Royalties via Public Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: The reward distribution function sends the calculated royalty fee to the caller (msg.sender) instead of a protocol-controlled address, allowing anyone to extract accumulated royalties.
threats.investigation_rationale: Protocol fees must be routed to designated treasury addresses, not to the arbitrary caller executing the distribution.
preconditions: CuratorRewardsDistributor has accumulated a non-zero balance for a credId

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Royalty fee is sent to msg.sender in a permissionless function
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever a credId has accumulated undistributed rewards

operational_questions: Is the royalty fee intentionally designed as a caller incentive?
Does the value of the royalty fee exceed the gas cost of calling distribute?
validation_requirements: Calculate the potential MEV value of the royalty fee
Test if a non-privileged account receives the royalty fee upon calling distribute

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor the CuratorRewardsDistributor for large deposits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: When royalty fee > gas cost, call distribute(credId)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Receive the royalty fee directly to the attacker's address
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: MEV bot infrastructure for optimal execution

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible - extracted ETH cannot be recovered

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent calls to distribute by MEV bots or unknown addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Protocol treasury not receiving expected royalties

threats.title: Continuous Denial of Service on Share Selling via Lock Period Reset
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can buy a single share on behalf of a victim, resetting the victim's 10-minute lock period and continuously preventing them from selling their shares.
threats.investigation_rationale: Time-based locks should only be triggered by the user's own actions, not by third-party deposits, to prevent griefing.
preconditions: Victim owns shares they intend to sell
Attacker has enough ETH to buy 1 share

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: buyShareCredFor updates the lock timestamp for the target address, not the caller

operational_questions: Can an attacker economically sustain this DoS attack?
Does batchBuyShareCred allow locking multiple creds for a victim simultaneously?
validation_requirements: Test buying 1 share for a victim and verify their sell transaction reverts
Calculate the cost of sustaining the attack over 24 hours

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a victim attempting to sell shares
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's lock period is reset to current block timestamp
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat every 9 minutes to permanently block the victim from selling

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Targeted users cannot exit their positions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires attacker to stop or run out of funds

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated purchases of 1 share for a specific address by a different address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users complaining about inability to sell shares

threats.title: Permanent Trapping of Excess ETH in Factory via Self-Call Context Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: When users send excess ETH to the claim function, the contract forwards only the exact fee to a self-call. The refund logic inside the self-call sends excess back to the factory itself, permanently trapping user funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Refunds must be routed to the original transaction sender (tx.origin or initial msg.sender), not the msg.sender of an internal self-call.

proof_of_signal.pattern: this.merkleClaim{ value: mintFee } followed by _msgSender().safeTransferETH in _processClaim

preconditions: User sends msg.value > required mintFee to claim or batchClaim

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: External self-call alters msg.sender context, breaking the refund mechanism

operational_questions: Does the factory contract accumulate ETH over time?
Is there any mechanism for users to recover trapped ETH?
validation_requirements: Test claim() with excess ETH and verify the user does not receive a refund
Verify the factory's ETH balance increases

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User accidentally or intentionally sends excess ETH to claim()
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: claim() calculates exact mintFee and calls this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Excess ETH remains in the initial claim() execution context
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: _processClaim refunds 0 ETH because inner msg.value == mintFee

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - owner can withdraw and manually refund, but requires off-chain coordination

threats.title: Forced Reward Withdrawal Griefing via Unprotected Access
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdrawFor function lacks access control, allowing any attacker to force the withdrawal of a victim's entire accumulated reward balance to the victim's address.
threats.investigation_rationale: Withdrawal functions should require authorization to prevent disruption of user strategies or forced interactions with reverting fallback functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdrawFor(address from, uint256 amount) external has no access control modifiers

preconditions: Victim has a non-zero reward balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: withdrawFor allows third parties to initiate state changes on behalf of others
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Anytime a user has a balance

operational_questions: Can this be used to disrupt smart contracts that rely on accumulating rewards?
Can this cause gas griefing if the target is a contract with an expensive receive function?
validation_requirements: Test calling withdrawFor(victim, 0) as an attacker
Verify victim's balance is forced to 0 and ETH is transferred

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a victim with a large accumulated reward balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The contract transfers the full balance to the victim
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's accounting or tax strategy is disrupted

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Disruption of user reward management strategies
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - funds are not lost, just moved prematurely

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: withdrawFor called by addresses other than the 'from' address

threats.title: Unreachable Upgrade Path for Deployed NFT Clones
threats.hypothesis_description: The PhiNFT1155 contract is UUPS upgradeable and restricts upgrades to its owner (the Factory), but the Factory contract lacks any function to trigger an upgrade, rendering the NFTs permanently non-upgradeable.
threats.investigation_rationale: Upgradeable proxy patterns require the authorized admin to have an exposed execution path to call upgradeToAndCall.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _authorizeUpgrade is onlyOwner (Factory), but Factory has no upgrade execution function

preconditions: A vulnerability is found in PhiNFT1155 requiring an upgrade

operational_questions: Was the intent to make NFT clones upgradeable?
Is there any hidden mechanism to execute calls from the Factory?
validation_requirements: Review Factory contract for any arbitrary call execution capabilities
Confirm clones are deployed as proxies (if not, UUPS is entirely dead code)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a critical bug in PhiNFT1155
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Protocol admins attempt to upgrade the contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Admins realize the Factory cannot call upgradeToAndCall
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Bug remains unpatched, leading to exploitation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to patch vulnerabilities in NFT contracts
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires migrating all users to new contracts

threats.title: Permanent Lock of Curator Rewards via Unbounded Distribution Loop
threats.hypothesis_description: The reward distribution function iterates over all curators for a cred in a single transaction. For popular creds, this will exceed the block gas limit, permanently locking all accumulated rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: Operations scaling with user count must implement pagination or pull-based mechanisms to avoid block gas limit exhaustion.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < distributeAddresses.length; i++) over unbounded array

preconditions: Cred has a large number of unique curators (e.g., > 1000)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unbounded loop over dynamic array of user addresses
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Once curator count exceeds gas limit threshold

operational_questions: What is the maximum number of curators before the transaction reverts?
Is it economically viable for an attacker to artificially inflate the curator count?
validation_requirements: Calculate gas cost per curator iteration
Determine the block gas limit on target chains (Base, Optimism)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies a cred with high accumulated rewards
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker uses multiple Sybil addresses to buy 1 share each
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Curator array grows beyond the block gas limit capacity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Legitimate calls to distribute() revert with Out of Gas

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade or hard fork

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large number of unique addresses buying minimal shares
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed distribute() transactions due to OOG

threats.title: Cross-Chain Replay of Credential Creation Signatures
threats.hypothesis_description: The signature payload for creating a cred does not include the chain ID, allowing an attacker to intercept a valid creation signature on one chain and replay it to create the same cred on another chain.
threats.investigation_rationale: Cryptographic signatures must be strictly bound to their intended execution domain (chain ID) to prevent cross-chain replay attacks.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol is deployed on multiple chains using the same signer address
threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts (Deploy.s.sol) show deployment to multiple chains with the same signer address (0x29C7...)

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.decode(signedData_, ...) does not extract or validate block.chainid

preconditions: Same signer key is used across chains

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing chainId in signature payload decoding
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After a cred is created on one chain

operational_questions: Does the off-chain signer include chainId in the signed hash?
Is the signer address identical across all deployed chains?
validation_requirements: Inspect the off-chain signing logic
Attempt to replay a Base signature on Optimism

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor mempool or recent blocks on Chain A for createCred transactions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Extract the signedData_ and signature_ from the transaction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Submit the exact same payload to the Cred contract on Chain B
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Cred is created on Chain B without authorization from the protocol

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Creation of unauthorized or duplicate credentials across chains
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires pausing and upgrading the contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Identical cred creation payloads appearing on multiple chains
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Creds appearing on chains where they were not intended

threats.title: Retroactive Locking of Transferable Assets via Soulbound Toggle
threats.hypothesis_description: The art creator can update the soulbound status of an art collection after tokens have been minted, allowing them to retroactively lock previously transferable assets or unlock previously soulbound ones.
threats.investigation_rationale: Asset transferability rules should be immutable post-minting to protect user property rights and secondary market expectations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: art.soulBounded = soulBounded_; applied globally to all tokens

preconditions: Art is created with soulBounded = false
Creator updates settings to soulBounded = true

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Global state variable controls transferability of already minted tokens

operational_questions: Is this an intended feature for creators to manage their collections?
Does this violate assumptions made by secondary marketplaces?
validation_requirements: Test minting a transferable token, updating settings, and attempting to transfer it
Verify if marketplaces handle dynamic soulbound status correctly

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Creator launches a highly anticipated, transferable NFT collection
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users mint and begin trading on secondary markets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Creator maliciously calls updateArtSettings with soulBounded = true
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All secondary market trading is immediately halted; user assets are locked

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - creator can revert the change, but trust is lost

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Calls to updateArtSettings changing soulBounded status
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden drop in transfer volume for a collection

threats.title: Permanent Trapping of Curator Funds via Unbounded Protocol Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: The protocol owner can set the protocol fee percentage to an arbitrarily high value (>100%). This causes the sell function to revert due to arithmetic underflow, permanently trapping all curator shares.
threats.investigation_rationale: Administrative fee setters must have hardcoded upper bounds to prevent accidental or malicious bricking of core economic functions.
preconditions: Admin key is compromised or acts maliciously

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing bounds check on critical economic parameter

operational_questions: Is the admin key secured by a multisig or timelock?
Can an accidental fat-finger input brick the protocol?
validation_requirements: Test setting fee to 10001 and observe sellShareCred revert
Review deployment scripts for admin key security

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker compromises the protocol owner key
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts due to underflow in price - protocolFee - creatorFee

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires admin to reset the fee
